text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Patient: Raymond Kowalski
DOB/Age: 63-year-old male
Diagnosis: Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), right kidney primary (pT3bN1M1), bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal metastases
Visit Type: Scheduled oncology follow-up -- first restaging assessment after completing ipi/nivo induction
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell RCC presenting for scheduled follow-up after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction immunotherapy and review of first restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed with stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney approximately 3 months ago. He presented initially with gross hematuria, right flank pain, and a 15-pound unintentional weight loss. Staging workup demonstrated an 11.4 cm right renal mass with extension of tumor thrombus into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary nodules. He was determined to be IMDC intermediate risk (2 risk factors: time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than one year, thrombocytosis). Pathology from both the pre-surgical core needle biopsy and the surgical specimen confirmed WHO/ISUP grade 4 clear cell RCC with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated VHL loss-of-function mutation, PBRM1 loss, BAP1 loss (the co-occurrence of BAP1 and PBRM1 loss being notable for its association with higher-grade biology and sarcomatoid features), SETD2 loss, MET amplification, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, and TP53 R248W gain-of-function mutation, among other alterations. PD-L1 (22C3) CPS was 18 on the core biopsy and CPS 22 in sarcomatoid areas on the nephrectomy specimen.

Given his resectable primary with oligometastatic disease, he underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 7 weeks prior to his first oncology visit post-surgery. Pathologic staging confirmed pT3b (renal vein thrombus), with 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive. He recovered well from surgery with resolution of hematuria and improvement of flank pain. Creatinine settled at approximately 1.6 mg/dL post-nephrectomy (from a baseline of 1.1 mg/dL).

Approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy, he was started on ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks. He completed 4 induction cycles over approximately 12 weeks without significant immune-related toxicity, with the notable exception of grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism that developed after cycle 2. TSH was 18.4 mIU/L with a suppressed free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL; he was started on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Repeat TSH 6 weeks later trended toward normalization. Immunotherapy was continued through all 4 cycles without dose modification.

He now presents for restaging review. The CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained this week demonstrated a mixed response: the right lower lobe pulmonary target lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm (approximately 36% reduction), and the right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.6 cm to 0.4 cm -- both clearly smaller. The left lower lobe nodule is stable at 0.8 cm. However, the retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal compartments show new or growing disease: a 1.6 cm right common iliac nodal mass not seen on prior imaging, and a new 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate (the prior individual para-aortic and aortocaval nodes measured 2.8 and 1.9 cm, but those were resected at the time of retroperitoneal lymph node dissection -- the new aortocaval conglomerate therefore represents recurrence/new nodal disease in the resected field). No hepatic, bone, or brain metastases are identified.

This discordant response -- pulmonary disease clearly improving while new nodal disease has appeared -- was discussed at length with the patient today. The differential for this pattern in the context of immunotherapy includes: (1) true discordant response, with immunotherapy effectively controlling the conventional clear cell component (which predominates in the lungs) but the sarcomatoid subclone driving nodal disease and proving less immunotherapy-sensitive in these nodal sites; (2) pseudoprogression, where immune infiltration into nodal metastases transiently enlarges them before tumor cell death; (3) early true progression at the nodal sites despite pulmonary benefit. Given that this is the first restaging scan, that pulmonary disease is clearly and meaningfully responding, and that the patient remains clinically well, the MDT consensus is to proceed cautiously before abandoning immunotherapy -- particularly given the established principle that checkpoint inhibitor responses can be heterogeneous, and abandoning effective therapy prematurely would be detrimental.

The patient reports mild fatigue (grade 1-2), which has been present since completing induction. He reports occasional nausea (grade 1) but is eating reasonably well. He denies fevers, chills, dyspnea, new musculoskeletal pain, abdominal pain, or neurologic symptoms. He denies rash, colitis symptoms, or visual changes. He endorses cold intolerance and mild fatigue consistent with his known hypothyroidism, though these symptoms have partially improved on levothyroxine. No lower extremity edema currently.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (grade 1-2), no fevers, no chills. Weight reported as stable over the last month (~180 lbs; was 183 lbs at last visit; down considerably from 198 lbs at diagnosis -- total weight loss approximately 18 lbs, much of which was prior to diagnosis).
Respiratory: No new dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis. Mild exertional dyspnea on stairs (unchanged from prior visit).
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema currently. Blood pressure has been well controlled per home readings.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea grade 1; appetite mildly decreased but tolerating oral intake. Normal bowel habits, no diarrhea, no colitis. No abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No hematuria (resolved post-nephrectomy). No dysuria. Urine output normal.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or joint pain that would suggest osseous metastases or inflammatory arthritis. No myalgias.
Neurologic: No headaches, diplopia, weakness, or cognitive changes.
Dermatologic: No rash or pruritus.
Endocrine: Cold intolerance improving on levothyroxine; no excessive sweating, tremor, or other thyroid symptoms.
Hematologic: No unusual bleeding or bruising.
Psychological: Anxious regarding today's mixed restaging results but coping reasonably well; engaged and asking good questions.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 98.2°F oral; heart rate 74 beats/minute; blood pressure 128/78 mmHg (left arm seated); respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute; oxygen saturation 97% on room air; weight 180.2 lbs.

General: Alert and oriented x 3. Well-nourished but mildly cachectic male appearing his stated age. In no acute distress. Pleasant and engaged.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive. Sclera non-icteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. Respirations unlabored at rest.

Abdomen: Well-healed right flank and midline surgical incisions without signs of infection, dehiscence, or hernia. Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds normoactive. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses on deep palpation.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No erythema or tenderness of hands or feet. Skin of palms and plantar surfaces intact without blistering or hyperkeratosis.

Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy appreciated (cervical, axillary, or inguinal).

Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.

Skin: No rash, vitiligo, or inflammatory skin changes. No ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 x10^9/L (reference 4.5-11.0)
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (low; recovering from post-surgical anemia, previously 10.8 g/dL)
- Platelets: 312,000/uL (normalized from 478,000 at diagnosis; thrombocytosis resolved post-nephrectomy)
- Differential: unremarkable; no lymphopenia

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 22 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL (stable, appropriate for single kidney status)
- eGFR: ~45 mL/min/1.73m^2 (stable CKD stage 3a, expected post-nephrectomy)
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (slightly elevated; consistent with known T2DM)
- ALT: 28 U/L (normal), AST: 24 U/L (normal), Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal), Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (normal) -- no evidence of hepatotoxicity or hepatic metastases
- Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL (normalized from 10.9 at diagnosis; hypercalcemia resolved post-nephrectomy)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (mildly low-normal)
- LDH: 198 U/L (improved from 287 U/L at diagnosis)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 3.9 mIU/L (normal, improved from 18.4 mIU/L at time of hypothyroidism diagnosis; on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal; previously suppressed)

Urinalysis: No hematuria, proteinuria trace (1+), no casts. (Proteinuria mildly present; will continue monitoring -- may be related to solitary kidney or early VEGF-pathway effect if switching therapy is needed)

HbA1c: 7.2% (suboptimal glycemic control; discussed with patient, metformin dose adjustments considered given creatinine -- will defer to primary care)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (current restaging, approximately 3 months post-initiation of systemic therapy):

As reviewed in detail with the patient today, this scan shows what we would characterize as a mixed/discordant response. On the positive side, the pulmonary target lesions have clearly shrunk: the right lower lobe nodule is down to about 9 mm (from 14 mm at baseline), and the right upper lobe nodule is down to 4 mm (from 6 mm). The left lower lobe nodule is essentially unchanged at 8 mm. These changes in the lungs are meaningful and tell us that nivolumab-based immunotherapy is working for at least part of this tumor.

On the concerning side, the retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal compartments are showing new or growing disease. There is a new 1.6 cm right common iliac nodal mass that was not present on baseline staging imaging. There is also a 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate -- this is a region that was surgically cleared at the time of cytoreductive nephrectomy and retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, so the appearance of new nodal disease here represents nodal relapse in that field. There are no new hepatic, osseous, adrenal, or intracranial findings. No new pulmonary nodules.

Overall interpretation: Mixed response with clear benefit in the lungs but new and concerning nodal disease below the diaphragm. This is a challenging clinical picture.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid differentiation, on nivolumab maintenance (cycle 1 of maintenance dosing, transitioning from ipi/nivo induction): MIXED RESPONSE on first restaging imaging.

The radiographic picture today is complex and warrants careful consideration. The pulmonary lesions have clearly responded to immunotherapy -- both the right lower lobe and right upper lobe nodules are measurably smaller, and this change exceeds what we would attribute to measurement variability. The left lower lobe nodule is stable. By standard criteria, the pulmonary component is in partial response.

However, new nodal disease has appeared in the right common iliac and aortocaval regions. The nature of this discordance is biologically plausible in the context of this particular tumor: his surgical specimen showed that 22% of viable tumor volume was sarcomatoid differentiation, and the sarcomatoid component had particularly high PD-L1 expression (CPS 22). While high PD-L1 is generally associated with immunotherapy benefit, sarcomatoid differentiation is also known to be associated with more aggressive biology and potentially heterogeneous immunotherapy responses. His NGS profile included both BAP1 and PBRM1 loss -- BAP1 loss in particular is associated with higher-grade tumors and aggressive natural history. It is possible that the conventional clear cell clones are responding in the lung while a genomically distinct clone is driving nodal progression.

Differential for this pattern:
- Pseudoprogression (immune infiltration causing transient enlargement of nodal metastases; the new nodes may regress on continued therapy)
- True discordant response (pulmonary disease benefiting; nodal disease progressing)
- Delayed immune response (nodes may ultimately respond; pulmonary benefit may be early signal of broader response)

Given that:
(a) This is the first restaging scan and we have not yet allowed adequate time to assess the full scope of the immunotherapy response;
(b) The pulmonary disease is clearly responding, which is encouraging and warrants continued anti-PD-1 therapy if safely possible;
(c) The patient is clinically stable with ECOG 1 performance status;
(d) The nodal disease, while new, is not causing critical organ compromise or rapid clinical deterioration;

...the MDT has reviewed this case and the consensus recommendation is to continue nivolumab maintenance and repeat cross-sectional imaging in 6 weeks. If nodal disease continues to grow or new metastatic sites appear at that interval, we will need to reconsider the treatment approach and likely transition to second-line TKI therapy (cabozantinib being the preferred agent given his MET amplification on NGS, which may predict enhanced sensitivity to MET-inhibitory VEGFR/TKI therapies).

Plan:
- Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (flat dosing maintenance)
- Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in 6 weeks for interval assessment of nodal disease
- If nodal progression continues at next scan: transition to second-line cabozantinib
- Patient counseled extensively today regarding this mixed response and the clinical rationale for continuing immunotherapy at this time despite the new nodal findings
- Patient understands and agrees with the monitoring plan

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism, grade 2 (on therapy):
- Currently well-managed on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
- TSH today 3.9 mIU/L -- appropriate response to thyroid replacement
- Continue levothyroxine at current dose
- Recheck TSH at next visit (in ~4 weeks)
- No dose adjustment needed at this time

3. Post-nephrectomy renal function / CKD stage 3a:
- Creatinine stable at 1.7 mg/dL (eGFR ~45 mL/min); expected and stable
- Trace proteinuria on UA today -- will monitor at follow-up
- Avoid NSAIDs; counsel on nephroprotective measures
- Metformin: per current guidelines, metformin can be continued cautiously at eGFR 30-45 (CKD stage 3b); patient is borderline -- discussed with primary care to consider dose adjustment

4. Hypertension:
- Blood pressure well-controlled today at 128/78 on lisinopril (dose not yet adjusted at this point in care)
- Continue current antihypertensive regimen
- Monitor blood pressure closely, particularly if TKI therapy is initiated in the future (cabozantinib and other VEGF pathway inhibitors significantly increase hypertension risk)

5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus:
- HbA1c 7.2% -- above target; discussed with patient
- Will defer dose adjustment of metformin to primary care given CKD considerations
- Encouraged healthy diet and modest physical activity as tolerated

6. Fatigue / anemia:
- Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL -- mild anemia, likely multifactorial (post-surgical, cancer-related, mild hypothyroidism)
- No transfusion indicated
- Fatigue is grade 1-2; patient coping; counseled regarding energy conservation, regular light exercise
- Thyroid replacement may continue to improve fatigue as TSH normalizes

7. Performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Patient is functional and managing at home without assistance.

8. Psychosocial:
- Patient is understandably anxious about the mixed imaging findings today
- Spent substantial time discussing the rationale for continued immunotherapy and the plan for close monitoring
- Offered connection with oncology social worker and/or patient support groups for RCC; patient interested
- Patient's family support appears strong (wife accompanied today)

Return to clinic: In approximately 4 weeks for nivolumab infusion and labs; repeat cross-sectional imaging in 6 weeks with clinic review at that time.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, greater than 50% of which was counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed -- Medical Oncology Attending]
